author: @samuelthomasengardio
text: The masonary drill bits are awesome. They make a diamond wheel for dremels to sharpen the carbide tips.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Awesome idea mate! I will try that. Cheers! :)

replies.author: @samuelthomasengardio
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey it makes a world of difference if you sharpen them before 1st use.

replies.author: @samuelthomasengardio
replies.text: I'd also like to add, that if you don't need the extra shaft length on the bit, chop it off. It will do you another favor, rigidity is a b word.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Cool, I usually use a wet diamond drill bit at medium speed making sure I keep an eye on ceramic temperature

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: That would be super ideal! I only had these masonry bits laying around though. Great suggestion, cheers mate! :)

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey totally understand about making do with what you have, I have been doing that most of my life. I found that if you ever see a cheap set that you can afford, even if poor quality and you go easy on them and they will serve you well. That's what I did anyway.

author: @PaddyNinja
text: Luke do you think ceramic magnets could be drilled out successfully in this way? Thanks m8.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Awesome question! To be honest I don't really know. I don't see why it wouldn't though. It is definitely worth a go. Cheers mate! :)

author: @henrybraithwaite5195
text: Diamond coated drill bits work so much better.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks for the tip mate! :)

replies.author: @henrybraithwaite5195
replies.text: @MyNameIsLukey “Diamond Cut Hole Saws” are the best. I’ve got a set, and I’ve yet to find something they won’t go through.

author: @johnoddvar1967
text: Nice job. I have been using special drils for ceramics, but those i not allways have. I think i shall try that out myself. Nice to lubricate with water.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: These were the only bits I had laying around. They did a great job though. Cheers mate! :)

replies.author: @samuelthomasengardio
replies.text: I told luke that they make a cheap diamond wheel for dremels that serve wonderfully to sharpen the carbide tips on these bits. I use these bits all the time for many purposes, I just chop them down. I never need the shaft length.

author: @charlesdickens6706
text: ......and circular diamond studded hole cutters down to small diameters are also available for ceramics ,glass , and stone .

author: @thornhedge9639
text: Nice job Luke.   By the way a drill bit for drilling glass works far better on ceramics than a masonry bit (using the same wet method you outlined)  The masonry bit is probably easier to find though (not to mention cheaper).

author: @htmagic
text: Luke, a diamond drill would also probably work. Put a dab of clay at the bottom of the hole to keep the water from running out everywhere.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Thanks for the tip mate! I didn't think about blocking the hole. Cheers!

replies.author: @dantronics1682
replies.text: I was going to suggest bluetack since its easier to get than clay

replies.author: @htmagic
replies.text: @dantronics1682 Either one works.

author: @edwardhughes352
text:
if you need a contimuous water supply, fill a 2 liter bottle with water and make a pin hole in the side near the bottom.
position the fine stream of water over the work. stop the stream by closing the lid of the bottle. you could position the work peice in a plastic container if you dont want  a wet  worltop. I have drilled nice holes in glass with this method using dimond hole  saws.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
with how brittle things like regular ceramic, aluminum oxide ceramic and tungsten carbide ceramics, I usually make a jig of something softer, that thing you are drilling would have been a "get drill bit the size of the whole piece, drill out a piece of wood, then cut the wood in half across the hole with the band saw, and clamp the two pieces back together in the vice around the ceramic part,) I do quite a bit of work on tungsten to make cutting components and a cheap source can be solid carbide bits from a supplier that is selling used units, as the whole shank becomes a chunk of stock (that I am using for making engraving chisels to carve tool steel for making dies with artwork, just chucked up in an electric engraving tool (not a rotary as this is a reciprocating or in and out action rather than a rotation action, so you just weigh against the back of the tool and each jab it takes puts more power than your hand so it just walks through cutting something like a wood chisel or the blade of a wood planer taking a flat layer or in some cases a tiny controlled curve or v shape if I am doing say a cheek bone or nose, or laying down hair lines or something,) that stuff is harder than your ceramic so using another piece of tungsten like you have in the tip of the drill bit, wont work, and you need diamond but I found it actually carves very well with diamond grit be it a flat sheet or shaped burr coated in diamond, I think because it does not stick around, at all, or make shavings or anything, the diamond hits that stuff and it turns right into dust and gets dropped to the bench, letting the diamonds reach fresh new carbide to scratch into as it goes, so it definitely makes for a much better process, 

one other thing I would say too, is using a tool that has a smoother running action so vibrations from the bit into the brittle structure of something like your ceramic nozzle looking item thus ensuring the bit bouncing around does not smack into it harder than you intended and break it. good steady hand though for sure that you were able to effect use in that drill, for sure, :)

author: @jamescunliffe9872
text: Is it a combustion chamber?:)

replies.author: @charlesdickens6706
replies.text: .....for the fun of it my guess is it's a crucible chemists and geologists use but it's almost like the old inkwells for school desks too.  You suggested Luke's making the tobacco chamber for a bong and its seems feasible to me . The borosilicate glass tubing will adhere to the ceramic nicely at temperature . Very creative indeed. The perfect project to hone those glass tubing skills necessary for a chemist lab .

replies.author: @jamescunliffe9872
replies.text: @charlesdickens6706 Yes that would be a elegant fusion solution, maybe blowtorch and microwave?

author: @synchro-dentally1965
text: Thanks for the video Luke. Ceramics can be very brittle. Would it have been advantageous to "pad" the vice grip with something to prevent the ceramic from potentially cracking?

replies.text: Yes, that is a great idea, it did not occur to me. But it will definitely help. Cheers mate! :)
